narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 50% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction is based on your profile, including your second-decile ranking in Year 1 grades—well below the typical student's mid-range position—your first-decile ranking in Year 3 grades, even lower than average, a LSAT score of 31 compared to the usual 37, and a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.4 that's actually stronger than most applicants' 3.2 average. Your middle family income bracket aligns with many others, you're a male applicant like over half in our dataset, white like the vast majority, and planning part-time work unlike the 93% who go full-time. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 academic decile, which had the biggest pull toward predicting failure, followed closely by your LSAT score exerting a similar strong influence in that direction. Your Year 1 decile came next in importance, also nudging the prediction toward failure, with family income playing a moderate role in the same way. Less influential but still contributing were your racial background, undergraduate GPA, part-time plans, and gender, all tilting slightly toward the risk of not passing. Compared to typical students who get approved, your lower high school deciles and LSAT stand out as bigger drags than your above-average college grades could offset, landing you right at that concerning 50% failure threshold. It's a close call, meaning small improvements could shift things dramatically. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 37, commit to full-time studies to match the successful majority, and build stronger study habits early to climb those deciles in any future academic settings. With your solid UGPA as a foundation, targeted prep for the bar could make all the difference next time. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas—many students turn predictions around with focused effort. Hang in there; this is just one data point in your journey.
